nativeRange.habitat: Generally found in shallow subtidal environments — rocky bottoms, reef rubble, oyster beds, sponges, and rock crevices — at depths of roughly 2–5 m. Tends to be cryptic and largely nocturnal in the wild.

diet.notes: Generally considered an opportunistic scavenger and meaty-food carnivore. Has been documented accepting mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped marine fish or shellfish flesh, sinking pellets, and quality frozen carnivore mixes. In an established reef tank it tends to pick at detritus, uneaten food, and decomposing material between feedings. Famous in the trade for consuming Aiptasia anemones, though individual results vary — well-fed shrimp may ignore Aiptasia altogether, and reliable Aiptasia control is most consistently reported for Lysmata boggessi rather than true L. wurdemanni.

avoidWith: predatory fish (lionfish, large wrasses, large dottybacks, hawkfish, triggerfish)
ornamental anemones (Entacmaea, Heteractis) — shrimp may be stung or eaten
any copper-based medication in the system

summary: The Peppermint Shrimp (Lysmata wurdemanni) is a small, translucent crustacean from the Western Atlantic, recognisable by the thin red stripes running along its body. Generally considered reef-safe and beginner-friendly, it is widely kept as a biological control for Aiptasia pest anemones — though reliable predation is most consistently associated with the closely related L. boggessi.
careNotes:
The Peppermint Shrimp is a small, peaceful, largely nocturnal shrimp from the tropical Western Atlantic — the US east coast, Gulf of Mexico, Florida Keys, and Bahamas. In the aquarium it tends to spend daylight hours tucked into caves, crevices, and shaded overhangs in the rockwork, emerging at feeding time and after lights-out to pick over the substrate for detritus, uneaten food, and small meaty items. A mature reef aquascape with ample hiding places and at least one open scavenging zone is generally advised.

Like all decapod crustaceans, Peppermint Shrimp are highly sensitive to copper-based medications and to swings in salinity, pH, or temperature. Drip-acclimation over 1–2 hours is generally advised on introduction. Iodine supplementation is sometimes recommended to support clean molts, though stable reef-tank parameters and good nutrition are typically sufficient; freshly molted shrimp are vulnerable and tend to hide for a day or two until the new exoskeleton hardens.

Generally considered reef-safe, with caveats: have been known to occasionally nip at large-polyp stony corals, zoanthids, or stressed/sessile invertebrates, especially if underfed. Compatible with most peaceful reef fish but should not be housed with predatory species that target shrimp (lionfish, large wrasses, large dottybacks, hawkfish, triggerfish, moray eels). Tends to be sociable with conspecifics and is commonly kept in small groups of 2–5; numbers should be matched to tank size and feeding regime, with extra shrimp often added when Aiptasia control is the goal.

Taxonomic note — species complex (NOT TO BE CONFUSED WITH): This entry documents Lysmata wurdemanni specifically — but the trade name 'Peppermint Shrimp' is applied indiscriminately across a cryptic-species complex, and the animal sold under that label is frequently not L. wurdemanni. A 2006 taxonomic revision split the historic 'Lysmata wurdemanni' into a complex including at least L. wurdemanni, L. rathbunae, L. ankeri, L. boggessi, L. pederseni, and L. bahia, with recent molecular work suggesting up to 9 species exist across the range. Visual identification requires close inspection of stripe patterns and antennal-segment counts. Reliable Aiptasia predation is most consistently associated with Lysmata boggessi, the Florida/Gulf species typically supplied to North American retailers. True L. wurdemanni has a more limited range (Atlantic US to Texas) and only rarely consumes Aiptasia. Hobbyists buying specifically for Aiptasia control are generally advised to request L. boggessi by name, as most retailers sell to the trade name and do not distinguish between members of the complex.

Family placement varies — older retailer pages list Hippolytidae, while current taxonomy places the genus in Lysmatidae. Adult size figures in the literature span roughly 5 cm to 7 cm, though aquarium specimens typically settle closer to the lower end of that range.
breedingNotes: Peppermint Shrimp are protandric simultaneous hermaphrodites, meaning individuals mature first as males and then begin functioning as both sexes at once — so any two adults can pair and produce eggs. Hatching of zoea larvae has been reported routinely in the aquarium, but raising the free-swimming larvae requires a dedicated system with gentle flow, no mechanical filtration, and live feeds such as rotifers and brine shrimp nauplii; this stage is considered challenging at the hobby scale. Lysmata boggessi is now commercially captive-bred, and captive-bred specimens are generally hardier and better adapted to aquarium life than wild-caught individuals.

additional.name: humble.fish — Peppermint Shrimp Identification
additional.notes: Visual ID guide differentiating Lysmata wurdemanni from the cryptic sister species in the Caribbean / Gulf complex (L. rathbunae, L. ankeri, L. boggessi, L. pederseni, L. bahia). Hobbyist-maintained reference recommended for confirming whether a 'Peppermint Shrimp' purchase is actually L. wurdemanni or one of the other complex members; informs the 'NOT TO BE CONFUSED WITH' framing in careNotes.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Generally molts roughly every 3–6 weeks under stable reef conditions; younger and faster-growing shrimp molt more frequently. The freshly molted shrimp is soft and vulnerable for about 24–48 hours and tends to hide; leave the shed exuvia in the tank for the shrimp to consume (recovers calcium and trace elements). Stable salinity and trace-element supplementation (iodine in particular) are commonly cited as supporting clean molts.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Egg-carrying females are common in the aquarium; rearing the planktonic zoea larvae is the bottleneck and typically requires a dedicated larval tank and live feeds.
